title: Robust Quantum Control via the Dark State: Adiabatic Population Engineering in a Three-Level Λ System
author: ALDAVA ASENCIOS, ROSARIO LIZETH
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Achieving high-fidelity quantum state engineering is pivotal for quantum technology applications. This work investigates the Stimulated Raman Adiabatic Passage (STIRAP) protocol in a three-level $\Lambda$-system to demonstrate robust and near-perfect population transfer ($\ket{1} \to \ket{2}$). The system evolution is rigorously modeled using the \textbf{Lindblad Master Equation} to account for realistic open quantum dynamics, specifically spontaneous decay from the intermediate state $\ket{3}$. We show that the \textbf{adiabatic evolution} through the Dark State, which is orthogonal to the decaying state $\ket{3}$, guarantees immunity to this dissipation, achieving fidelities $> 0.99$. Furthermore, we analyze the protocol's resilience, mapping the fidelity landscape in the parameter space and confirming STIRAP's intrinsic robustness against control amplitude variations. This study demonstrates the efficacy and practical viability of STIRAP as a robust method for coherent population transfer in realistic noisy quantum devices.
steps_raw: Achieving high-fidelity quantum state engineering is pivotal for quantum technology applications. This work investigates the Stimulated Raman Adiabatic Passage (STIRAP) protocol in a three-level $\Lambda$-system to demonstrate robust and near-perfect population transfer ($\ket{1} \to \ket{2}$). The system evolution is rigorously modeled using the \textbf{Lindblad Master Equation} to account for realistic open quantum dynamics, specifically spontaneous decay from the intermediate state $\ket{3}$. We show that the \textbf{adiabatic evolution} through the Dark State, which is orthogonal to the decaying state $\ket{3}$, guarantees immunity to this dissipation, achieving fidelities $> 0.99$. Furthermore, we analyze the protocol's resilience, mapping the fidelity landscape in the parameter space and confirming STIRAP's intrinsic robustness against control amplitude variations. This study demonstrates the efficacy and practical viability of STIRAP as a robust method for coherent population transfer in realistic noisy quantum devices.
